course_subtitle: The principles of the Open Compute Project are ultimately realized in physical metal, power, and fluid.
course_description_html:
The principles of the Open Compute Project are ultimately realized in physical metal, power, and fluid. In this module, you will explore the flagship hardware standards of OCP, examining the complete "Grid to Chip" integrated system.

From the physical architecture of the Open Rack Version 3 (ORv3) to the revolution in advanced liquid cooling, you will learn exactly how the community engineers hardware to survive the extreme thermal densities of modern artificial intelligence.

blocks.body_html:
Entering the Data Center

The principles of the Open Compute Project are ultimately realized in physical metal, power, and fluid. In this module, you will explore the flagship hardware standard of OCP: The Open Rack Version 3 (ORv3).

You will also analyze why the rise of artificial intelligence and heat-dense workloads has forced the industry to transition from traditional air cooling to advanced cooling technologies.

buttons.label: Data Center Basics
buttons.description_html:
Hungry for more Data?

Check our course series to enhance your understanding of Data Center Basics and unlock new opportunities in the field.

blocks.heading_html: By the end of this module, you will be able to:

items_html: Examine the physical architecture and power delivery system of ORv3.
Understand the engineering physics behind the shift from 12V to 48V power.
Analyze advanced thermal management solutions, including Cold Plate and Immersion cooling.
Identify broader OCP standards including Facility Readiness, Sustainability, and Systems Management.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
Before we can look at the software or the facility, we must understand the physical rack itself.

Dive into the engineering mechanics of the Open Rack Version 3 and see how the community redesigned the standard data center cabinet to handle massive AI payloads.

Click play to watch the video.

blocks.quote_html: "With the Open Rack Wide, we are not just making room for bigger silicon; we are making room for the future."

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
The standard ORv3 frame handles today's AI workloads beautifully, but the OCP Community is already engineering for tomorrow.

Workgroups are actively developing the High Power Rack (HPR) shared baseline power shelves and the Open Rack Wide specification, which widens the frame to safely support the extreme weight and networking density of next generation AI clusters.

items.title: 48V DC Busbar
items.description_html: The spine of the rack. By distributing 48V DC power instead of legacy 12V, the ORv3 significantly reduces power distribution losses, enabling the rack to efficiently support high-density compute gear.

items.title: Blind-Mate Power Connectors
items.description_html: Allows IT gear (servers, storage sleds) to connect directly to the busbar simply by sliding them into the rack. This eliminates the "spaghetti" of power cables at the back of the rack, drastically reducing service time.

items.title: Centralized Power Shelf
items.description_html: Instead of every individual server having its own redundant power supply unit (PSU), ORv3 uses a centralized power shelf to convert AC grid power to 48V DC for the entire rack. This reduces stranded power and improves overall facility efficiency.

items.title: IT Gear & Frame
items.description_html: A standardized, vanity-free frame designed to maximize airflow and support extreme physical weight, accommodating the heavy servers required for modern AI clusters.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
The standard ORv3 frame handles today's AI workloads beautifully, but the OCP Community is already engineering for tomorrow. Workgroups are actively developing the High Power Rack (HPR) shared baseline power shelves and the Open Rack Wide specification, which widens the frame to safely support the extreme weight and networking density of next generation AI clusters.

Click play to watch the video.

buttons.label: View Specifications
buttons.description_html:
See the Thermal Engineering at Scale

Review the official HPR and ORW specifications to see exactly how these advanced frames integrate direct liquid cooling and massive manifolds.

buttons.label: OCP Academy catalog
buttons.description_html:
Explore the Future of Cooling

DLC is the primary cooling technology that the market is adopting. For an overview of liquid cooling and more deep dives into DLC, fluids and two-phase DLC, see the OCP Academy catalog of technology courses.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
Now that you understand the physical limits of thermal solutions, let us explore the actual technologies the OCP Community is deploying to solve it.

While traditional air cooling remains the standard for standard cloud workloads, the industry is rapidly adopting advanced liquid solutions to manage the extreme heat of high density AI.

Click through the tabs below to understand the progression of thermal management, from standard air systems to full dielectric immersion.

items.title: Air Cooling (The Legacy Limit)
items.description_html: Traditional data centers push chilled air through the front of the server and exhaust hot air out the back. While effective for standard cloud workloads, it becomes physically and economically impossible to use air to cool racks exceeding 20kW to 30kW.

items.title: Direct-to-Chip (Cold Plate)
items.description_html: Fluid is brought directly inside the server. Coolant Distribution Units (CDUs) pump fluid through specialized manifolds and UQD blind mate connectors directly into small metal cold plates mounted on the CPUs and GPUs. This captures up to 80% of the heat at the source.

items.title: Rear Door Heat Exchangers (RDHx)
items.description_html: A transitional technology. A liquid-filled coil is attached to the rear door of the rack. As hot air leaves the servers, it passes through the coil and is immediately cooled before entering the data center room. It allows facilities to handle higher densities without changing the server hardware itself.

items.title: Immersion Cooling
items.description_html: Entire servers are submerged in a tank of dielectric fluid. The fluid absorbs 100% of the heat emitted by the hardware. It requires a complete redesign of the data center floor but offers unparalleled efficiency.

buttons.label: OCP Open DC for AI
buttons.description_html:
Is Your Facility Ready for High Density Power and Cooling?

Transitioning to higher powers and the liquid cooling it necessitates requires specific facility infrastructure.

To understand more about the facility-level requirements for AI data centers, follow the Data Center Facility project and Open Data Center for AI initiative.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
The graphic below illustrates the evolution of heat transfer. As we move from traditional air cooling toward liquid cooling, we dramatically reduce the reliance on standard air as a heat transfer medium.

Direct to Chip (D2C) and Immersion technologies bypass the air almost entirely, using water or dielectric fluid to extract heat directly from the components, offering the massive thermal capacity required for high density AI.

As you can see, D2C cooling delivers the most efficient targeted heat extraction by capturing the vast majority of the thermal load, while keeping economic and environmental costs on the low end.

blocks.caption_html: As per January 2025, based on average use cases data*

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html:
The ORv3 and liquid cooling manifolds are vital, but a true "Grid to Chip" philosophy requires standardizing everything around the rack as well.

Click through the tabs to see how OCP extends into the facility and the software layer.

items.title: Data Center Facilities (DCF)
items.description_html: The DCF project focuses on optimizing the physical infrastructure and power distribution beyond the rack level.

items.title: Sustainability
items.description_html:
OCP demonstrates a strong commitment to sustainability and circularity.

The sustainability groups have developed a standardized disclosure label for embodied carbon in products. This allows operators to accurately track and manage the environmental impact of their hardware choices.

items.title: Systems Management
items.description_html:
Managing physical infrastructure and operational technology, from the grey space to the white space, requires robust open management solutions. OCP has standardized Redfish profiles for IT equipment and OT equipment.

For example, CDUs have open  management profiles created by the Hardware Management project, but they can be refined and extended by the CDU project's community.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.body_html: Test your knowledge from this module. Select the best answer for each question, then submit.

blocks.question_html: Why did the Open Rack standard shift from a 12V busbar (ORv2) to a 48V busbar (ORv3)?

choices.text_html: To reduce power distribution losses for high-density AI workloads.
To make the racks lighter and easier to ship.
To eliminate the need for liquid cooling.

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! The Open Rack standard shifts from a 12V busbar (ORv2) to a 48V busbar to reduce power distribution losses for high-density AI workloads.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. The Open Rack standard shifts from a 12V busbar (ORv2) to a 48V busbar to reduce power distribution losses for high-density AI workloads.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

blocks.question_html: Which cooling technology involves mounting a liquid-filled plate directly onto the CPU or GPU?

choices.text_html: Immersion Cooling
Rear Door Heat Exchangers (RDHx)
Direct-to-Chip (Cold Plate)
Raised Floor Air Cooling

blocks.feedback_correct_html: Correct! Direct-to-Chip (Cold Plate) involves mounting a liquid-filled plate directly onto the CPU or GPU.
blocks.feedback_incorrect_html: Not quite. Direct-to-Chip (Cold Plate) involves mounting a liquid-filled plate directly onto the CPU or GPU.

blocks.label: CONTINUE
blocks.complete_hint: Complete the content above before moving on.

items_html: The ORv3 represents a leap in rack architecture, centralizing power delivery and utilizing a 48V busbar to dramatically increase efficiency.
Blind-mate connectors and a vanity-free frame eliminate cable clutter and maximize airflow.
As AI workloads push rack densities past 100kW, OCP is leading the transition to advanced liquid cooling, including Direct to Chip and Immersion technologies.
OCP standards extend far beyond the rack, driving innovations in facility readiness (DCF), carbon disclosure, and open systems management.

blocks.body_html: You know the history, the community, and the technology. In our final module, Today and Tomorrow, we will look at how you can take action. We will guide you through the OCP Marketplace and discuss the future of sustainable infrastructure.
